title: Contrabass violin

| G4–D5–A5–E6, tuned an [octave](/wiki/Octave "Octave") above the [violin](/wiki/Violin "Violin"), like the [Kit violin](/wiki/Kit_violin "Kit violin") or pochette[[1]](#cite_note-1) or the two "kleine Poschen" of the [Syntagma Musicum](/wiki/Syntagma_Musicum "Syntagma Musicum") of [Michael Praetorius](/wiki/Michael_Praetorius "Michael Praetorius")[[2]](#cite_note-2) | About the size of a 1⁄4 violin

| C4–G4–D5–A5, tuned a [fourth](/wiki/Perfect_fourth "Perfect fourth") above the [violin](/wiki/Violin "Violin"), like the [violino piccolo](/wiki/Violino_piccolo "Violino piccolo") | About the size of a 3⁄4 violin

| G3–D4–A4–E5, tuned the [same](/wiki/Unison "Unison") as the [violin](/wiki/Violin "Violin") | The body is slightly longer (368–381 mm; 14.5–15.0 in) than a conventional violin (355 mm; 14.0 in) but the strings are about the same length (328–338 mm; 12.9–13.3 in)
| C3–G3–D4–A4, tuned the [same](/wiki/Unison "Unison") as the [viola](/wiki/Viola "Viola") | Similar to a viola but larger, and is often played upright, like a cello

| G2–D3–A3–E4, tuned an [octave](/wiki/Octave "Octave") below a standard [violin](/wiki/Violin "Violin"), like the historical [tenor violin](/wiki/Tenor_violin "Tenor violin") | The body is similar to a 1⁄2 cello but with thinner ribs and a longer neck
| C2–G2–D3–A3, tuned the [same](/wiki/Unison "Unison") as a [cello](/wiki/Cello "Cello") | Larger than a conventional cello

| A1–D2–G2–C3, tuned a [fourth](/wiki/Perfect_fourth "Perfect fourth") above a [double bass](/wiki/Double_bass "Double bass") *or* G1–D2–A2–E3, tuned a [fourth](/wiki/Perfect_fourth "Perfect fourth") below a cello.[[3]](#cite_note-3) | About the size of a 5⁄8 double bass; played like a double-bass or sitting down (like a cello)

| E1–A1–D2–G2, tuned the [same](/wiki/Unison "Unison") as a [double bass](/wiki/Double_bass "Double bass") *or* C1–G1–D2–A2, tuned an [octave](/wiki/Octave "Octave") below a cello. | Larger than a conventional double bass; played like a double bass

is a family of stringed instruments developed in the 20th century primarily under the direction of the American [luthier](/wiki/Luthier "Luthier") [Carleen Hutchins](/wiki/Carleen_Hutchins "Carleen Hutchins"). Each instrument is based directly on the traditional [violin](/wiki/Violin "Violin") and shares its acoustical properties, with the goal of a richer and more homogeneous sound. Unlike the standard modern stringed instruments, the main resonance of the body of the violin octet instrument is at a pitch near the two middle open strings, giving the instruments a more balanced, clearer sound.
which were however three string instruments
two types are shown both having three strings: see [Syntagma Musicum, vol. 2, page XXI](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/8/8e/Syntagma12.png)

The existence of two basses, a smaller one and a larger one, was also a feature of the [viol](/wiki/Viol "Viol") family, which had two types of [violone](/wiki/Violone "Violone") tuned one fourth apart

(Redirected from [Contrabass violin](/w/index.php?title=Contrabass_violin&redirect=no "Contrabass violin"))

String instrument family

Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Violin octet"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Violin+octet%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Violin+octet%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Violin+octet%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Violin+octet%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Violin+octet%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Violin+octet%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(October 2023)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Violin octet

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| From left to right:  * Baritone violin * Small bass violin * Contrabass violin * Tenor violin * Alto violin * Mezzo violin * Soprano violin * Treble violin | |
| Other names | The New Violin Family |
| [Classification](/wiki/Musical_instrument_classification "Musical instrument classification") | * [Bowed string instruments](/wiki/Bowed_string_instrument "Bowed string instrument") |
| [Related instruments](/wiki/Musical_instrument "Musical instrument") | |
| * [Violin](/wiki/Violin "Violin") * [Viola](/wiki/Viola "Viola") * [Cello](/wiki/Cello "Cello") * [Double bass](/wiki/Double_bass "Double bass") | |

The **violin octet** is a family of stringed instruments developed in the 20th century primarily under the direction of the American [luthier](/wiki/Luthier "Luthier") [Carleen Hutchins](/wiki/Carleen_Hutchins "Carleen Hutchins"). Each instrument is based directly on the traditional [violin](/wiki/Violin "Violin") and shares its acoustical properties, with the goal of a richer and more homogeneous sound. Unlike the standard modern stringed instruments, the main resonance of the body of the violin octet instrument is at a pitch near the two middle open strings, giving the instruments a more balanced, clearer sound.

The instruments were proposed by composer [Henry Brant](/wiki/Henry_Brant "Henry Brant") in 1957 and the first octet was completed in 1967.

## Instruments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Violin_octet&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Instruments")]

The instruments of the violin octet are:

| Instrument | Range | [Tuning](/wiki/Musical_tuning#Basic_tuning_(open_strings) "Musical tuning") | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Treble violin |  | G4–D5–A5–E6, tuned an [octave](/wiki/Octave "Octave") above the [violin](/wiki/Violin "Violin"), like the [Kit violin](/wiki/Kit_violin "Kit violin") or pochette[[1]](#cite_note-1) or the two "kleine Poschen" of the [Syntagma Musicum](/wiki/Syntagma_Musicum "Syntagma Musicum") of [Michael Praetorius](/wiki/Michael_Praetorius "Michael Praetorius")[[2]](#cite_note-2) | About the size of a 1⁄4 violin |
| Soprano violin |  | C4–G4–D5–A5, tuned a [fourth](/wiki/Perfect_fourth "Perfect fourth") above the [violin](/wiki/Violin "Violin"), like the [violino piccolo](/wiki/Violino_piccolo "Violino piccolo") | About the size of a 3⁄4 violin |
| Mezzo violin |  | G3–D4–A4–E5, tuned the [same](/wiki/Unison "Unison") as the [violin](/wiki/Violin "Violin") | The body is slightly longer (368–381 mm; 14.5–15.0 in) than a conventional violin (355 mm; 14.0 in) but the strings are about the same length (328–338 mm; 12.9–13.3 in) |
| [Alto violin](/wiki/Vertical_viola "Vertical viola") |  | C3–G3–D4–A4, tuned the [same](/wiki/Unison "Unison") as the [viola](/wiki/Viola "Viola") | Similar to a viola but larger, and is often played upright, like a cello |
| [Tenor violin](/wiki/Tenor_violin "Tenor violin") |  | G2–D3–A3–E4, tuned an [octave](/wiki/Octave "Octave") below a standard [violin](/wiki/Violin "Violin"), like the historical [tenor violin](/wiki/Tenor_violin "Tenor violin") | The body is similar to a 1⁄2 cello but with thinner ribs and a longer neck |
| [Baritone violin](/wiki/Baritone_violin "Baritone violin") |  | C2–G2–D3–A3, tuned the [same](/wiki/Unison "Unison") as a [cello](/wiki/Cello "Cello") | Larger than a conventional cello |
| Small [bass violin](/wiki/Bass_violin "Bass violin") |  | A1–D2–G2–C3, tuned a [fourth](/wiki/Perfect_fourth "Perfect fourth") above a [double bass](/wiki/Double_bass "Double bass") *or* G1–D2–A2–E3, tuned a [fourth](/wiki/Perfect_fourth "Perfect fourth") below a cello.[[3]](#cite_note-3) | About the size of a 5⁄8 double bass; played like a double-bass or sitting down (like a cello) |
| Contrabass violin |  | E1–A1–D2–G2, tuned the [same](/wiki/Unison "Unison") as a [double bass](/wiki/Double_bass "Double bass") *or* C1–G1–D2–A2, tuned an [octave](/wiki/Octave "Octave") below a cello. | Larger than a conventional double bass; played like a double bass |

Note: While the *Small Bass* and *Contrabass* violins were designed and originally meant to be tuned in fifths, most bassists find the required extra shifting impractical. The former[[4]](#cite_note-4) [New Violin Family Association](http://www.nvfa.org/) listed tunings for these instruments in fourths, while the Hutchins Quartet retains the original fifths tuning.

## Bows

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Violin_octet&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Bows")]

Carleen Hutchins did not design specific bows for the instruments of the violin octet. This is important research which still has not been completed.[*[according to whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] Players of these instruments use a variety of violin, viola, cello and double-bass bows, looking for the best fit they can at the moment.

## Performing groups

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Violin_octet&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Performing groups")]

Currently, there are three performing groups which play and record on the instruments of the violin octet.[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Violin_octet#Performing_groups "Talk:Violin octet")*] The Hutchins Consort (based in [San Diego, California](/wiki/San_Diego,_California "San Diego, California")) plays on Carleen Hutchins' instruments. The Hutchins Consort Quartet is a subset of the consort and plays on soprano violin, tenor violin, baritone violin and contrabass violin. The Albert Consort (based in [Ithaca, New York](/wiki/Ithaca,_New_York "Ithaca, New York")) uses a set of instruments made by Robert Spear and the New Violin Family Orchestra, organized by the association [Octavivo](http://octavivo.org/) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20130516110940/http://octavivo.org/) 2013-05-16 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine"), which also uses instruments made by Robert Spear.

## Use in traditional roles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Violin_octet&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Use in traditional roles")]

The instruments of the violin octet do not necessarily have to be used in the context of the consort and for playing music written especially for them. They can also be used as alternatives to members of the usual [violin family](/wiki/Violin_family "Violin family"): for example any [string quartet](/wiki/String_quartet "String quartet") could be played by an ensemble consisting of two mezzo violins, one alto violin and one baritone violin, as an alternative to the two violins, viola and cello of the usual string quartet.

The best-known use of a member of the New Violin Family in this sort of role is that of an [alto violin](/wiki/Vertical_viola "Vertical viola") by [Yo-Yo Ma](/wiki/Yo-Yo_Ma "Yo-Yo Ma") to perform and record [Béla Bartók](/wiki/B%C3%A9la_Bart%C3%B3k "Béla Bartók")'s [Viola Concerto](/wiki/Viola_Concerto_(Bart%C3%B3k) "Viola Concerto (Bartók)").
